coredata.dc:description: The Ti-MIL-125 metal-organic framework (MOF) functions as a nanoreactor, producing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within its structure and forming CQD/MOF composites. Similarly, a Pt-CQD/MOF three-component composite is synthesized. Their properties, along with those of CQDs and MOF-free-CQDs (mf-CQDs) enclosed within the MOF, undergo assessment and comparison. Integrating CQDs and Pt minimally alters the MOF structure but reduces specific surface areas due to incorporation. The emitted wavelength of CQD/MOF (570 nm) differs from pristine CQDs (450 nm) and mf-CQDs (496 nm), indicating MOF influence on CQDs' growth and photoluminescence. HOMO and LUMO levels of CQDs/MOF, measured via ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy and low-energy inverse photoelectron spectroscopy, are more positive than those of the MOF, suggesting heterogeneous junction formation, aiding electron separation. CQD/MOF exhibits a modest photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of 20.4 μmol/g/h. Conversely, Pt-CQD/MOF significantly boosts the rate to 2213 μmol/g/h, underscoring Pt's pivotal catalytic role.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of the composites of carbon quantum dots and metal organic framework and their application in hydrogen evolution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Ti-MIL-125 metal-organic framework (MOF) functions as a nanoreactor, producing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within its structure and forming CQD/MOF composites. Similarly, a Pt-CQD/MOF three-component composite is synthesized. Their properties, along with those of CQDs and MOF-free-CQDs (mf-CQDs) enclosed within the MOF, undergo assessment and comparison. Integrating CQDs and Pt minimally alters the MOF structure but reduces specific surface areas due to incorporation. The emitted wavelength of CQD/MOF (570 nm) differs from pristine CQDs (450 nm) and mf-CQDs (496 nm), indicating MOF influence on CQDs' growth and photoluminescence. HOMO and LUMO levels of CQDs/MOF, measured via ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy and low-energy inverse photoelectron spectroscopy, are more positive than those of the MOF, suggesting heterogeneous junction formation, aiding electron separation. CQD/MOF exhibits a modest photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of 20.4 μmol/g/h. Conversely, Pt-CQD/MOF significantly boosts the rate to 2213 μmol/g/h, underscoring Pt's pivotal catalytic role.

ce:section.ce:para: For semiconducting materials, when their sizes are decreased to several to tens of nanometers, they become quantum dots (QDs) [1,2]. Due to the confinement effect of the QDs, their physical and chemical properties differ from those of their bulk material [3]. In addition, the properties of the QDs can be modified effectively by the confinement effects, which can be controlled by their sizes, functional groups, surface modification, surface ligands, doping elements, defects, etc. [4]. For instance, the QDs usually have bandgaps larger than the bulk material and, furthermore, the smaller the size, the higher the bandgap [5]. Regarding the properties of QDs, the optical and electronic characteristics have been extensively studied, aiming to fabricate QDs with high light absorption, bright fluorescence and controllable emission wavelength [6–10]. By tuning the quantum confinement effects, QDs with excellent optical properties had been prepared, enabling them to be applied in biological labeling, photovoltaic, photocatalysts, sensing, fluorescent probes, light-emitting diodes, displays, and so on [7–10].

Instead of semiconductor QDs, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted a great deal of attention recently because they are considered to be alternatives to substitute for traditional quantum dots (QDs) containing toxic heavy metals. CQDs not only have good optical and physicochemical properties of semiconductor QDs, but also possess the advantages of tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties, high stability, biocompatibility, easy synthetic routes, abundant precursors and low cost [11–31]. Based on the specific properties of the CQDs and their composites, a variety of applications, including light emitting devices [23], bioimaging [20], fluorescence sensing [21], cancer therapy [13,15,22], photocatalysis [18], hydrogen generation [14,17,27,28], hydrogen peroxide production [29], carbon dioxide reduction [30,31] etc., are studied continuously.

Although CQDs have a lot of merit, controlling the properties of CQDs, especially the emission wavelength and quantum yield (QY), for specific applications is an issue that needs to be studied. In the literature, various methods and precursors have been utilized to synthesize CQDs, and QY over 60% had be obtained [23,32–35]. However, the high QYs were obtained only for CQDs with short emission wavelengths (green, yellow). The QYs of CQDs with red emission light are still low. Therefore, many researchers have focused on the fabrication of red emission CQDs.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have drawn a lot of attention in recent years [36,37]. MOFs have open network structures organized by transition metal ions and organic linkers, which possess unique chemical properties, exceptional numerous micro to mesopores, and high surface area [38]. Due to these unique properties, MOFs have been applied in a variety of fields, including gas storage [39], adsorptions [40], ophthalmology [41], catalyses [42], molecular separations [43], sensing [44] and so on. One of the other advantages of MOFs is that their structures and properties can be tuned by various chemical compositions [38] In addition, the frameworks possess a degree of flexibility due to their hybrid composition; this allows the dynamic transport of guest material into or across the matrix. While MOFs have many advantages and the discoveries of new MOFs and their properties are reported continuously, they still have shortages. Therefore, MOF-based composites were developed by combination with foreign material to overcome their intrinsic deficiencies.

The MOF composites are prepared by immobilizing nanoscale materials or functional molecules into the frameworks. Two methods, termed “ship in the bottle” and “bottle around ship” have commonly been utilized for the fabrications [45–48]. For the first one, the pre-prepared MOFs were dispersed in a solution consisting of the precursors of the desired material (i.e., CQD). The precursors penetrate through the pore windows of MOFs into the framework. The precursors then cause an in-situ reaction inside the matrix, transferring to the desired material [47,48]. On the contrary, for the process of “bottle around ship”, the nanoparticles (i.e., CQD) or functional material were prepared first and used as templates for the synthesis of MOFs. In existing literature, diverse materials and composites based on MOFs [49–52], notably, CQDs/MOFs composites, have been employed in various specific photocatalytic reactions [53–56]. In the realm of photocatalytic reactions, the investigation into generating hydrogen from water captures significant interest, primarily due to the potential of hydrogen as an environmentally friendly energy carrier, promoting a sustainable energy future.

To enhance the efficiency of hydrogen production, MOFs and their composites are frequently paired with a small quantity of co-catalyst. For instance, platinum (Pt) nanoparticles (NPs) attract photogenerated electrons, and a sacrificial electron donor contributes its electron to the photogenerated holes, safeguarding the MOF from degradation.

In the endeavor to develop CQD composites and verify their application in photocatalytic hydrogen generation, this study centered around preparing CQD/MOF composites. This was achieved by introducing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as guest materials into Ti-MIL-125 MOFs. For embedding the CQD into the pores of MOFs, the “ship in the bottle” process was employed, allowing the precursors to penetrate into the matric of MOFs. The properties of CQD/MOF, as well as those of the CQDs prepared with and without the presence of MOFs were characterized and compared. Finally, the CQD/MOF and its Pt co-catalytic composite (Pt-CQD/MOF) were applied for the efficient hydrogen evaluation reaction.

The chemicals utilized in this work were supplied by J.T. Baker (Ethanol (99.9%), Methanol (99.8%), Hydrochloric acid (36.5–38%) and Citric acid (CA, 99.5%)); Alfa Aesar (Hexachloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6•6H2O, 99.95%); Sigma-Aldrich (o-Phenylenediamine (o-PD, 99.5%), tetra-n-butyl titanate (TBT, 97%)); DUKSAN (Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.9%)); Thermo scientific (Terephthalic acid (H2BDC, 99%)). All the regents were used as received without further purification.

The synthesis was performed by a modified method reported in a previous study (Fig. S1a) [57]. In brief, 1 g H2BDC, 36 ml DMF and 4 ml methanol were mixed by sonication and stirring. During the mixing process, 0.52 ml TBT was added drop by drop; the solution then was moved to an autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 20 h. After the reaction, a centrifugation process was used to separate the MOFs and liquid solution. Finally, the residual molecules on the solid sample were removed several times by anhydrous methanol. Finally, the sample was dried in an 80 °C oven and activated in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h before being stored in a low-humidity desiccator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQD/MOF composites
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, citric acid and o-phenylenediamine were utilized as precursors to prepare CQDs and the relative composites by solvothermal method using ethanol as the solvent. For the synthesis of CQDs, 100 mg of citric acid and 100 mg o-phenylenediamine were added into 10 ml ethanol. The solution was mixed by a sonication process for 10 min and then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave to perform the reaction at a temperature of 150 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the solution after the reaction was subjected to silica column chromatography using ethyl acetate as the eluent. Finally, solid CQDs could be obtained by removing the eluent solvent using a rotavapor.

The preparation process of CQD/MOF composites was similar to that of CQDs (Fig. S1b). The main difference is that, in addition to the citric acid and o-phenylenediamine, 100 mg Ti-MIL-125 was also added into the precursor solution. After the reaction, the solution was centrifuged to get rid of the solution from the CQD/MOF; the collected CQD/MOF were then repeatedly washed using ethanol to remove the residual compounds on their surface. The final step, similar to that of MOFs, the sample was dried in an 80 °C oven and activated in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Pt/MOF and Pt-CQD/MOF composites
ce:section.ce:para: For preparing Pt and CQD co-incorporated composite (Pt-CQD/MOF), 60 mg CQD/MOF was mixed with a solution consisting 15.6 ml MeOH and 6 ml H2PtCl6 (0.016 M) (Fig. S2). The solution was bubbled with N2 for 30 min in a vial and then illuminated by Xe lamp (300 W) under stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, after centrifugation, the solid products were washed repeatedly by methanol solution (71%) to remove the residual precursor. The solution adhered to the products were then replaced by repeated dipping in acetone (2, 8 and 2 h). Finally, the sample was dried in an 80 °C oven and activated at 60 °C in a vacuum oven for 12 h. The method for preparing Pt/MOF composites is identical to that of the Pt-CQD/MOF, except that the solid added into the methanol solution is MOF, not CQD/MOF.

To perform the hydrogen evolution, methanol was used as the scarifying agent, and 0.1 mg photocatalyst was added into a solution containing 2 ml methanol and 3 ml water. First of all, the air in the container was removed by bubbling with Ar for 12 h. The photocatalytic reaction was then carried out by illumination of light from a Xenon lamp (300 W) equipped with filter to simulate the sunlight of AM 1.5 G (100 mW cm−2). The solution was stirred at 500 rpm for 12 h and the evolved H2 was collected and analyzed by gas chromatography (GC).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the materials obtained by X-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses
ce:section.ce:para: First of all, Ti-MIL-125 MOFs (termed as MOFs here after) were prepared and the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was measured and shown in Fig. S3. It shows that the characteristic peaks of the MOF synthesized here are identical to those of the simulated ones, indicating that the Ti-MIL-125 MOF was successfully prepared. Then, by using the “ship in the bottle” method, the MOFs were introduced into the precursor solution for CQD synthesis and the reaction was controlled at 150 °C for 2 h. The precursor molecules were expected to penetrate the matrix of MOFs and perform the reaction there, forming CQD inside the MOF. This composite is termed CQD/MOF hereafter. The CQD/MOF was subjected to XRD analysis. According to the spectra shown in Fig. S3, no obvious difference in the spectra was observed between the MOF and CQD/MOF, indicating that the structure of the MOF was not destroyed after the CQDs synthesis. In the following step, the CQD/MOF were dispersed in a methanol solution containing H2PtCl4, which was then illuminated by Xe lamp to perform the reduction reaction to obtain the Pt anchored CQD/MOF. This composite was termed Pt-CQD/MOF. Fig. S3 shows that 2θ values of 39.8, 46.2, 47.5, 67.5 is related to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystalline planes, respectively for the (Pt) nanoparticles. The XRD spectrum of Pt-CQD/MOF in the 2θ range of 5°–40° is nearly identical to those of the MOFs and CQD/MOF. These results indicate that the structure of the MOFs did not change due to the synthesis processes and the incorporation of CQDs and Pt.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis (images of the MOF, CQD/MOF and Pt-CQD/MOF are shown in Fig. 1 . It appears that all the three compounds demonstrate a disk-like shape, and the average size and cross-section thickness are ca. 0.7 μm and 0.5 μm, respectively. The surface of bare MOFs is smooth, but it becomes rougher after incorporation of CQDs and Pt, especially for the Pt-CQD/MOF This phenomenon results from the interaction between MOF and either CQD or Pt, leading to the formation of surface pores on MOF. These pores facilitate the growth of CQD within MOF's cavity or the inclusion of Pt. Even though the CQD/MOF and Pt-CQD/MOF lose their original surface morphologies, the XRD results indicate that their structure is still preserved.

The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was used to measure the specific surface area of these materials. The adsorption isothermals of MOF, CQD/MOF and Pt-CQD/MOF show that the adsorptions of nitrogen on these materials follow the Langmuir adsorption type (Fig. 2 a). Accordingly, the specific surface area measured for MOF is 1520 m2g-1, which is smaller than the theoretical specific surface area of Ti-MIL-125 (2140 m2g-1), but similar to the value reported in the literature (1351–1550 m2g-1) [58]. For the CQD/MOF and Pt-CQD/MOF, the specific areas decrease to 1240 m2g-1 and 950 m2g-1, respectively, indicating that the formation of CQDs and Pt into the MOFs decreases the specific surface area of MOFs, which also implies that the pores of MOF were filled and/or blocked by CQD and Pt. For the internal pore sizes shown in Fig. 2b, no apparent difference was found for the three materials, and the sizes are ca. 0.93 nm, implying that the original structure of MOF was not destroyed by incorporation of CQD and Pt, which is consistent with the results of the XRD spectra.

The compositions of these materials were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and the results are demonstrated in Fig. S4 and Table 1 . For the spectrum of Ti-MIL-125, peaks of, Ti 2p, C 1s and O 1s were observed and their atomic compositions measured were 8.8%, 57.4% and 33.8%, respectively. Regarding the CQD/MOF, an additional peak (N 1s) appears; furthermore, the compositions of Ti and O decrease, while that of C increases, indicating the incorporation of CQD into the MOF. When Pt was further filled into the CQD/MOF, a significant peak corresponding to Pt 4f was observed and the corresponding composition was 9.8%. Furthermore, the composition of C decreased notably in comparison to that of the CQD/MOF, which may be ascribed to the substitute of Pt for CQD. However, a complicated interaction among the three materials can be confirmed. To evaluate the interaction among the CQD, MOF and Pt in the composites, a detailed scan of the XPS analysis was performed; and the spectra of C, Ti and O are shown in Fig. 3 . By de-convolution the peaks of C (Fig. 3a–d & g), three peaks: C-C/C=C (284.7 eV), C-O (285.9) and C=O/O=C-O (288.8 eV) could be found for all three materials, indicating that the C atoms have similar bonding states in these materials. The results also indicate that incorporation of CQD and Pt did not affect the interaction of C to the atoms around. However, the fractions (peak area) of the bonding states were changed. For the spectra of Ti, the peaks are nearly symmetry and the binding energies are almost the same in the three materials (Fig. 3 b, e & h). The symmetry peak indicates that the Ti atoms have only one chemical state and, furthermore, the three materials have similar states. Regarding the spectra of O, they are more complicated, as shown in Fig. 3 c, f & i. Three states: Ti-O (529.8 eV), C=O (531.3 eV) and C-O (533.1 eV) can be assigned in the three materials. It shows that the main state of O presents as Ti-O, and the C-O bond is always the least one. But in the CQD/MOF, the O in Ti-O and C-O states are similar. As Pt is included, the fraction of Ti-O increases obviously, but that of C-O state becomes much smaller. The high variation of O states in the three materials may be responsible for their different properties. In Fig. S5, two prominent peaks are observed at 70.5 eV and 73.8 eV, corresponding to Pt 4f 7/2 and 4f 5/2, respectively. Upon closer examination, two chemically distinct Pt entities are discernible in the spectrum. These entities are identified as metallic Pt (0), characterized by binding energies (BE) of 70.5 and 73.9 eV, and oxide Pt2+ with BE values of 71.4 and 74.9 eV. Notably, the predominant state of Pt species is metallic, with the minor presence of Pt2+ species possibly stemming from the incomplete reduction of Pt entities. These species may form bonds with oxygen atoms, likely due to the adsorption of oxygen on the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of the materials
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the optical characteristics of materials incorporating MOF, UV-visible spectroscopy analysis was performed to examine the absorbance properties of MIL-125 (Ti), CQD/MOF, and Pt-CQD/MOF composites. The UV-visible absorbance spectra, depicted in Fig. 4 , reveal insights within the wavelength range of 200–700 nm. In Fig. 4, the absorbance spectrum of MIL-125 (Ti) exhibits a peak below 350 nm, with no apparent absorption peak in the visible region. This observation suggests that MIL-125 (Ti) lacks energy utilization within the visible-light spectrum. In contrast, the CQD/MOF composite displays an absorption peak associated with MIL-125 (Ti) and an additional peak with a maximum absorption wavelength ranging from 400 to 600 nm. Meanwhile, the Pt-CQD/MOF spectrum reveals an augmentation in visible light absorption within the 350–800 nm wavelength range. The UV-visible analyses align with the visual appearance of the samples under UV light, as illustrated in Fig. S6. The PL spectra, illustrated in Fig. 4b, were examined to assess the recombination and separation rate of photoelectron-hole pairs in the photocatalysts. Typically, a decrease in PL intensity signifies a reduced recombination rate of electrons and holes, indicating enhanced photocatalytic activity. Upon comparing the spectra of these samples, it is evident that the PL intensity of Pt-CQD/MOF is notably low. This low PL intensity indicates that Pt-CQD/MOF boasts high charge separation efficiency and enhanced photocatalytic activity.

To clarify whether distinctions exist in the optical properties between CQDs and CQD/MOF, the CQD/MOF was treated in an alkaline solution to destroy the structure of the MOF. The solution was then nuturalized by acid and separated by filtration to get a solution containing CQDs. Finally, it was subjected to a column chromatography process to obtain high purity CQDs. The MOF-free CQD is termed mf-CQD hereafter. For comparison, CQDs synthesized in the absence of MOF was also prepared. These CQDs were dispersed in an ethanol solution for analysis.

Fig. 5 a shows the UV-vis absorption spectra of CQDs and mf-CQD, as well as that of the solid CQD/MOF for comparison. It shows that for the two CQDs in ethanol solution both π - π * and n -π * transitions can be observed, but the n -π * transition peak of mf-CQD has higher intensity and longer wavelength (ca. 440 nm) than the CQD (410 nm). The PL spectra shown in Fig. 5b indicate that the emitting wavelengths are 496 and 450 nm, respectively for mf-CQD and CQD. These emitting wavelengths are independent of the wavelengths of exciting light (Fig. S7) and the QYs measured are 36.6 and 30.0% for mf-CQD and CQD, respectively. The PL properties of these CQD and CQD/MOF are summarized in Table 2 .

Figs. S8a and b show the photographs of CQD and mf-CQD under room light. The two CQDs have a similar color (yellow), but that of mf-CQD is much deeper. Upon the illumination of UV-light, blue and green fluorescence light were displayed for CQD and mf-CQD, respectively (Fig. S8 c & d. These results indicate that the CQD imbedded in MOFs (i.e., in CQD/MOF) has different properties with those separated from the MOFs (i.e., mf-CQD). Furthermore, the properties of mf-CQDs also differ from the CQDs synthesized in a system without MOF.

The CQDs and mf-CQDs were observed by transmission electron microscope and their images are shown in Fig. 6 . The average size of mf-CQD measured according to the images is ca. 2.14 nm, which is smaller than the size of CQD prepared without MOF (3.03 nm) and the value reported in the literature [35,59]. It is inferred that the MOF limits the growth of CQD because the synthesis was performed inside the frameworks of MOFs.

For applying the materials in H2 evolution, methanol was used as the scarifying agent and 0.1 mg of the material was added into a solution containing 2 ml methanol and 3 ml water. Then, the air in the container was removed by bubbling with Ar. An Xe lamp (300 W) equipped with filter was used to simulate the sunlight of AM 1.5 G (100 mW cm−2) for performing the photocatalyst reaction. The reaction was prolonged for 12 h and the evolved H2 was collected and analyzed by GC. Fig. 7 shows the H2 evolution rate for various materials. It shows that if only MOF was used, the evolution rate is negligible. For the CQD/MOF, the rate is also small (20.4 μ mole h−1 g−1). These results differ from those reported for NH2-MIL-125(Ti), which has a higher H2 evolution rate; furthermore, incorporation of CQD demonstrated an enhancing effect to the evolution rate [60] The result obtained may be ascribed to the different properties of the two MOFs and CQD/MOF composites. Two possible reasons were proposed for the low evolution rate of Ti-MIL-125: (1). The short light absorption range (<350 nm) as shown in Fig. 5a; (2). The low activity of the MOF to hydrogen reduction.

For the CQD/MOF, the light absorption range increased to ca. 550 nm, but the activity of CQD and/or energy levels of the two materials were not mismatched. Pt is known as a good catalyst for hydrogen evolution; therefore, Pt was embedded into the MOF, forming a Pt/MOF composite to perform the evolution reaction. Fig. 7 shows that the rate increased significantly to 1561 μ mole g−1 h−1, confirming the catalytic effect of Pt. To further increase the evolution rate, the three components composite, Pt-CQD/MOF, was applied. For the three composites synthesized at different temperatures, the one prepared at 150 °C had an even higher evolution rate of 2213 μ mole g−1 h−1, which is high than the one prepared at 120 Co (1568 μ mole g−1 h−1) and 180 Co (1421 μ mole g−1 h−1) (Fig. S9). The solar-to-hydrogen-conversion efficiency and the apparent quantum yield calculated for the Pt-CQD/MOF composite were 3.06% and 2.43%, respectively [64]. Apparently, the synergistic effects of the three materials led to the significant increase in the hydrogen evolution rate. According to the performance and the organization of the materials, these effects may consist of the following mechanisms. (1). The MOFs act as the template of CQDs and/or Pt; thus two or three of the materials can be contacted, as demonstrated in Fig. S10. The contact of the materials may form a heterojunction, enhancing the electron separation. (2). The high catalytic activity of Pt is a critical property in performing this reaction. For the CQD/MOF, although the light absorption range extends to ca. 550 nm, sparse excited electrons can be utilized to reduce the hydrogen ions but release the energy as fluorescence light. This inference was confirmed by the fluorescence property of CQD/MOF, shown in Fig. S6c. (3). For the Pt-CQD/MOF, its black color indicates that it has a wider light absorption range and higher absorption ability. Furthermore, the disappearance of fluorescence property indicates that the excited electrons transferred to the Pt, which led to the increase in the hydrogen evolution rate.

The cycle test of the Pt-CQD/MOF composite on the photocatalytic H2 production was carried out to evaluate their reusability. During this test, the Pt-CQD/MOF composite was washed and dried after every run. The corresponding results up to 4 cyclic tests are given in Fig. S11. The performance of the Pt-CQD/MOF composite was decreased with the repeated cycles. At the fourth cycle, the H2 evolution rate drops to a value ca. 50% of the rate of the first cycle. To investigate the decline in performance of the Pt-CQD/MOF composite, XRD analysis was conducted after the completion of the fourth cycle test. Comparing the obtained XRD spectrum with that of the fresh catalyst (Fig. S12), it did not show apparent different, suggesting that the structure of the composite didn't change throughout the reaction. Consequently, the decrease in the hydrogen evolution rate was inferred to be due to weak interaction among CQD, Pt and MOF, or the catalytic poison effect.

Since the CQDs were embedded in the MOF, the CQDs were expected to form a heterojunction with the MOFs. To figure out the related energy levels, ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) and low energy inverse photoemission spectroscopy (LEIPS) were utilized to measure the HOMO and LUMO of MOF and CQD/MOF; the spectra are shown in Figs. S13 and S14. The analysis reveals that the HOMO and LUMO values obtained from UPS and LEIPS for TiML-125 are 1.58 eV and −1.29 eV (versus NHE) [61]. Consequently, the band gap of TiML-125 is calculated to be 2.87 eV. In contrast, the HOMO and LUMO values for CQD/MOF are determined to be 1.183 eV and −0.897 eV, respectively, resulting in a corresponding band gap energy of 2.080 eV. Since the CQDs, MOFs and Pt may contact together in the composite, a structure was also proposed for Pt-CQD/MOF, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . The narrower band gap of the CQD/MOF compared to the MOF makes it more adept at capturing light for catalytic reactions, especially crucial for the hydrogen evolution process where Pt plays a vital role as a catalyst. The excited electrons on the conduction band of CQDs can transfer directly to Pt, performing the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, since a heterojunction interface was formed between CQDs and MOFs, the excited electrons can also transfer to the MOFs. These electrons, as well as those of the light excited ones of MOF, will transfer to Pt and perform the hydrogen evolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison among the H2 evolution performances of CQD, MOF and Pt-based composites in the literature
ce:section.ce:para: Gu et al. utilized thermal polymerization to synthesize nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon quantum dots and g-C3N4 composite (NSCQDs/CN). CQDs were introduced into the CN to enhance the photocatalytic activity of CN [62]. Employing NSCQDs/CN as photocatalysts and Pt particles as co-catalysts, a hydrogen evolution rate of 5033 μmol h−1g−1 was reported using triethanolamine (TEOA) as a sacrificial agent. This rate was approximately 8.3 times higher compared to pure CN.

In a study of Hareesh group [63], TiO2 was functionalized with nitrogen and phosphorus CQDs (NPCQD/TiO2). The catalyst exhibited a hydrogen evolution rate of 533 μmol h−1 g−1. In 2022, Sk et al. utilized NH2-MIL-125 MOF, polyaniline and cobalt oxide to prepare Co3O4@PANI@NH2-MIL-125 [64]. This composite exhibited a photocatalytic hydrogen production rate of 1208 μmol h−1 g−1, which is approximately two-fold increase compared to the rate obtained by PANI@NH2 -MIL-125@Pt.

In an alternate study, Chen et al. synthesized the copper (Cu(I)/Cu(II)-amino-functionalized Ti(IV) metal-organic framework (Cu-NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)) [65]. This catalyst achieved a hydrogen evolution rate of 490 μmol h⁻1 g⁻1, demonstrating a 27 and 10-fold increase over NH2-MIL-125 and Pt-NH2-MIL-125, respectively. The higher catalytic activity primarily resulted from the elevated carrier density and prolonged lifetime of photogenerated charges in the presence of the Cu-NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) catalyst.

In 2021, Gong and colleagues introduced the stable cerium-based MOF (Ce-TTCA) for photocatalytic reactions [66]. This catalyst, accompanied by a Pt co-catalyst was utilized to perform photocatalytic hydrogen production, and evolution rates of 60.3 and 348.8 μmol h⁻1 g⁻1, were achieved at pH = 2 and 12, respectively.

Au and Pt incorporated zirconium 1, 4-dicarboxy benzene (UiO-66)-MOF-based composites such as AuAg24@UIO-66-NH2, PtAg24@UIO-66-NH2, and UiO-66-NH2@Pt@UiO-66-H were prepared by various research groups [67,68]. Among these, AuAg24@ UIO-66-NH2 exhibited a high hydrogen production rate of 3576 μmol h−1 g−1 [67]. The high catalytic activity in photocatalytic hydrogen production was attributed to the incorporation of AuAg24 into the MOF structure, which created a favorable charge transfer pathway that promoted charge separation. This, coupled with optimized Ag electronic states, underpinned the effectiveness of the catalytic process.

Aksoy et al. incorporated uniform Pt nanoparticles into or onto graphitic carbon nitride (gCN)/amorphous tungsten oxide (gCN/a-WOx), which featured wrinkled paper-like amorphous phase WOx (a-WOx) materials in multiple oxidation states over the gCN nano sheets [69]. This catalyst was employed for the photocatalytic hydrolysis of ammonia borane, providing a turnover frequency value of 419.1 mol H2 Pt−1 min−1, which exceeded that of gCN/Pt nanocatalysts (287.7 mol H2 mol Pt−1 min−1). In a subsequent study, Song et al. synthesized 1D nickel phosphonate-loaded 2D-g-C3N4 via hydrothermal synthesis [70]. Nickel phosphonate served as co-catalysts for photocatalytic water splitting. The CN/NiPPh-1 composite showed improved separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes compared to pristine CN. Optimal loading of NiPPh at 1 mmol yielded a hydrogen production rate of 261.0 mmol h−1 g−1, four times higher than pristine g-C3N4. In a recent development, Xiao et al. achieved a uniform distribution of Pt nanoparticles across the surface of the palladium porphyrin MOF (PCN-222(Pd)) [71]. The Pt/PCN-222(Pd) exhibited a high hydrogen production rate of 3932 μmol h−1 g−1, which was 2.2 times higher than that of PCN-222(Pd).

In this study, the synthesis of composite materials, including CQD/MOF and Pt-CQD/MOF, was successfully achieved using the "ship in the bottle" method. The hydrogen evolution rate for Pt-CQD/MOF reached 2213 μmol g⁻1 h⁻1, notably exceeding that of Pt-free CQD/MOF (20.4 μmol g⁻1), as well as surpassing values reported in other literature [62–67,69], as demonstrated in Table 3 .

In summary, composite materials comprising CQD/MOF and Pt-CQD/MOF were successfully synthesized using the "ship in the bottle" process. This method involved incorporating CQD precursors, such as citric acid and o-phenylenediamine, into the Ti-MIL-125 MOF. Incorporating CQDs and Pt into the MOFs did not induce structural changes in the MOFs. Nevertheless, a notable reduction in specific surface areas was observed, especially in the Pt-CQD/MOF composite. This decline is ascribed to the inclusion of CQDs and Pt, filling the frameworks of the MOFs. The mf-CQD was additionally produced by extracting the MOF component from the CQD/MOF composite. Despite the different colors observed in MOF, CQD/MOF, and Pt-CQD/MOF, only CQD/MOF demonstrated a significant PL peak at 575 nm, setting it apart from the PL peaks of CQD and mf-CQD. This distinction can be attributed to the cage effects of MOFs influencing the growth of CQDs and the PL mechanism. In contrast to MOF, the mf-CQD displays a reduced band gap, coupled with positive LUMO and HOMO potentials. These features imply the creation of a heterogeneous junction at the CQD/MOF interface, thereby improving electron separation. In contrast to MOF, mf-CQD exhibited a narrowed band gap along with positive LUMO and HOMO potentials. These characteristics suggest the formation of a heterogeneous junction at the CQD/MOF interface, thereby enhancing electron separation. However, concerning photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, the evolution rate for CQD/MOF is modest at 20.4 μ mol g⁻1 h⁻1. Nevertheless, this rate can significantly increase to as much as 2213 μ mol g⁻1 h⁻1 when Pt-CQD/MOF is employed, highlighting the pivotal role of Pt in this catalytic reaction.
